Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Bernard Bolzano

1781 – 1848

Propositions in themselves and the ideal contents of judgment — before phenomenology, before logic as we know it.

Bernard Bolzano did not write a single modern treatise called “philosophy of mind.” What he did leave is something arguably more interesting: a network of doctrines in logic, epistemology, metaphysics, religion, ethics, and the theory of science from which a fairly systematic philosophy of mind can be reconstructed. The reconstruction has to be careful, because Bolzano’s mind-theory is not a Cartesian psychology, not a Kantian transcendental theory of consciousness, not a Brentanian intentional psychology, and not a modern physicalist philosophy of mind. It is a hybrid: anti-psychologistic about logic and meaning, realist about mental acts, metaphysical about souls, empirically sensitive about cognition, and interactionist about body and mind.

The central thesis is this: Bolzano separates objective content from subjective mental occurrence more sharply than almost anyone before Frege and Husserl, while still treating mental acts as real events or properties of individual souls. Logic is not psychology; but psychology is indispensable for explaining how finite minds grasp, judge, infer, err, perceive, will, and act.

Bolzano, born in Prague in 1781, was a Catholic priest, mathematician, logician, philosopher, and professor of philosophy of religion; he is now often treated as one of the major logicians between Leibniz and Frege, and his major philosophical works include the Theory of Science of 1837 and the Textbook of the Science of Religion of 1834. His philosophy of mind has to be reconstructed especially from the Theory of Science, the Athanasia on the immortality of the soul, his metaphysical doctrines of substance and adherence, and his ethics and philosophy of religion.

1. The architecture: three levels, not one

Bolzano’s philosophy of mind becomes intelligible only if we distinguish three domains.

First, there are objective, nonmental entities: propositions in themselves, ideas in themselves, truths in themselves, logical relations, and grounding relations. These are not words, not thoughts, not brain states, and not acts of consciousness. Bolzano’s anti-psychologism consists precisely in refusing to reduce logic to mental processes. His “pure logic,” in a broad sense, includes not just formal inference but epistemology and philosophy of science; yet its basic objects are not psychological. Commentators often describe this as Bolzano’s opening of a “World 3” of purely logical objects.

Second, there are subjective mental phenomena: subjective ideas, subjective propositions, judgments, sensations, feelings, desires, volitions, acts of inference, acts of belief, and acts of knowing. These are real occurrences in individual minds. They happen in time. They arise and cease. They are causally connected with other mental and bodily events. Bolzano calls such items mental processes or phenomena, and commentators describe them as properties or “adherences” of a thinking being, mind, or soul.

Third, there is the metaphysical bearer of mental life: the soul or mind as a real simple substance. Bodies are aggregates of substances; a living organism is an organized aggregate in which one substance “rules” or coordinates the whole. In a rational being, this ruling substance is the soul or mind. Bolzano’s metaphysics therefore places psychology inside a broader theory of substances, forces, bodies, and causal interaction.

This three-level architecture is the key to avoiding several misreadings. Bolzano is not a psychologistic logician, because propositions and truths do not depend on being thought. He is not a eliminativist about the mental, because actual thoughts and judgments are real properties of individual souls. He is not a simple Cartesian dualist, because soul and body are not two utterly heterogeneous substances of mind-stuff and matter-stuff; both are treated under the same general ontology of real substances and their properties. But he is also not a materialist, because the soul is not reduced to body or brain.

A compact formula would be: objective content is nonmental; subjective cognition is mental; the mind is a real simple substance capable of grasping objective content.

2. Bolzano’s anti-psychologism and its importance for mind

Bolzano’s most important contribution to the philosophy of mind is paradoxical: he begins by refusing to make logic depend on mind. A proposition in itself is not a sentence and not a thought. It is what may be expressed by a sentence and grasped by a thought. A truth in itself is a true proposition whether or not anyone knows it, thinks it, or asserts it. Bolzano’s examples and definitions stress that objective propositions and ideas are “grasped” by mental phenomena and “expressed” by linguistic phenomena, but are rigorously distinct from both.

This move has enormous consequences for a philosophy of mind. It gives Bolzano an early version of the act/content distinction. A person’s judging that snow is white is an event in that person’s mind. But the proposition that snow is white is not identical with that event. Many people can judge the same proposition. One person can judge the same proposition at different times. The proposition is not multiplied by the number of mental episodes that grasp it.

Bolzano’s position thus anticipates a central problem in later philosophy of mind: how can a private, temporal, causally embedded mental episode have public, repeatable, truth-evaluable content? His answer is not to identify the content with the episode, nor to identify it with a linguistic inscription, but to say that the episode stands in a primitive “grasping” relation to an objective idea or proposition.

This “grasping” relation is basic. Bolzano does not reduce it to causation. Objective propositions do not physically cause our thoughts. Yet a mental act can grasp a proposition, and once it does so, it becomes contentful. This gives Bolzano a non-naturalistic theory of intentional content embedded inside a naturalistic or quasi-naturalistic theory of mental occurrence. The occurrence is real and causal; the content is objective and noncausal.

That dual structure is one reason Bolzano matters historically. Later analytic philosophers would distinguish sentence, thought, proposition, belief-state, and truth-condition. Phenomenologists would distinguish act, content, object, and evidence. Bolzano’s system already contains the pressure that leads to these later distinctions.

3. The basic mental items: ideas, judgments, thoughts

Bolzano’s most important mental categories are subjective ideas and subjective judgments.

An objective idea, or idea in itself, is a component of propositions. A subjective idea is a real mental phenomenon by which a mind grasps an objective idea. A subjective proposition or thought is a mental occurrence by which a mind entertains an objective proposition. A judgment is not merely entertaining a proposition; it is taking or asserting the proposition as true.

This gives Bolzano a layered account of cognition:

A mind can merely have an idea.

A mind can combine ideas into a thought of a proposition.

A mind can entertain a proposition without affirming it.

A mind can judge the proposition to be true.

A mind can believe or know, depending on further epistemic status.

The distinction between merely thinking a proposition and judging it is particularly important. It means that Bolzano does not reduce cognition to representation. There is also judgmental force: the act of affirmation, the taking-to-be-true. This anticipates later distinctions between propositional content and assertoric force, and between entertaining a content and believing it.

Bolzano’s mental ontology is also token-based. Actual thoughts are real events or properties of actual minds. They occur in time and enter causal relations. The same objective proposition may be present in many mental tokens, but the tokens themselves are individual, dated, and private. In modern terms, Bolzano combines objective propositional realism with mental-act realism.

4. Mind, soul, and substance

Bolzano’s language of “soul” and “mind” must not be treated as an accidental theological residue. It is structurally important. In his metaphysics, everything real is either a substance or an adherence. A substance is a bearer of properties. An adherence is an attribute or property of a substance or of another adherence. Mental phenomena such as feeling, desire, volition, and thought are adherences of an individual mind.

The soul is the substance that bears mental adherences. It is simple, not an aggregate. The body, by contrast, is an aggregate of substances. In living organisms, Bolzano treats one substance as “ruling” or coordinating the aggregate; that ruling substance is the soul of the organism, while the rest of the aggregate is its body. In rational beings, the soul is the mind or spirit. A specialist study of Bolzano’s philosophy of mind notes that he does not sharply separate “soul” and “mind”: “soul” names the substance, while “mind” emphasizes the soul’s rational or mental function.

This is not Cartesian dualism in the strict textbook sense. Descartes distinguishes thinking substance and extended substance as two fundamentally different ontological kinds. Bolzano’s ontology is instead monadological or substance-theoretic: both bodies and souls are built into a general theory of simple substances, forces, and adherences. The difference between soul and body is not that one belongs to a different metaphysical universe, but that the soul is the ruling simple substance of a living aggregate and bears the relevant mental properties. Bolzano inherits something from Leibnizian monadology, but he rejects Leibniz’s windowless monads and the doctrine of pre-established harmony; he assumes interaction among finite atoms or monads.

This makes Bolzano an interactionist. The soul and body are causally related. The body can affect the soul, as in perception; the soul can affect bodily action, as in volition. But because body and soul are both treated as real substances within a unified ontology, the interaction problem has a different shape from the Cartesian problem. Bolzano does not have to explain how an immaterial thinking substance can interact with an extended material substance if these are conceived as two utterly heterogeneous categories. He must instead explain how simple substances, some of which rule aggregates and bear mental properties, interact through forces.

That explanation remains metaphysically underdeveloped by contemporary standards, but it is not naïve. Bolzano explicitly treats objections concerning the soul’s relation to body, including questions about whether the soul occupies a place in the body, whether interaction with the body would make it corporeal, and whether a soul that represents composite things must itself be composite. These issues appear in the structure of the Athanasia, his work on the immortality of the soul.

5. Incorporeal minds, immortality, and the soul

Bolzano’s most explicit treatment of the soul as such is the Athanasia, first published anonymously in 1827 and later in a second edition in 1838 under the title Athanasia oder Gründe für die Unsterblichkeit der Seele—that is, “Reasons for the Immortality of the Soul.” The work is not merely a devotional text. It attempts a rational metaphysical argument for the soul’s persistence.

The core argument, as reported by the standard secondary literature, is that the soul is a simple substance, and simple substances cannot begin or cease to exist. If successful, this would establish the immortality of the soul. However, Bolzano’s own proof is not generally regarded as successful; the SEP notes that his proof is deficient.

For philosophy of mind, the significance is nevertheless substantial. Bolzano’s mind is not an epiphenomenal function of the body. Nor is it a merely abstract bundle of representations. It is a real simple substance capable of bearing mental states. If the soul survives bodily death, then Bolzano’s system allows for finite incorporeal or disembodied minds: minds no longer organized around their former bodily aggregates, yet still real substances capable, at least in principle, of mental adherences.

Bolzano’s view of incorporeal mind also includes God, though God occupies a special place. Bolzano’s ontology distinguishes real objects from objective propositions and ideas; he also includes God as a real being outside space and time. This means God is not a proposition in itself, not an abstract logical item, and not a human mental act. God belongs, in Bolzano’s ontology, to reality, though not to spatio-temporal reality in the ordinary finite sense.

The soul doctrine is also broader than a narrowly human psychology. The Athanasia discusses animal and plant souls, and even subordinate souls of organic parts. The digitized text contains passages indicating that every animal and plant may be said to have its own soul, and that bodily parts or organs may have subordinate souls that can in some sense deviate from the rule of the whole. This is not equivalent to modern panpsychism, because Bolzano is not simply attributing consciousness to all matter. But it does show that “soul” for him is not merely a synonym for human reflective consciousness. A soul is a ruling or organizing substance of a living aggregate; rational mind is a special case.

This broader organismic doctrine matters. It means that Bolzano’s philosophy of mind has a biological dimension. Mind is not introduced only at the level of adult rational self-consciousness. Mentality is connected with life, organization, ruling forces, sensation, and action. Human rationality is continuous with a wider metaphysics of living beings, even though rational souls have distinctive capacities for judgment, inference, morality, and science.

6. Representation and intentionality

Bolzano’s theory of representation is one of the most important bridges between his logic and his psychology.

Objective ideas are not mental images. They are not private pictures. They are abstract components of propositions. A mental representation is a subjective occurrence that grasps such an objective idea. Bolzano thus avoids a common empiricist picture in which ideas are little inner images, and he also avoids the Kantian view that content is constituted by the forms and categories of the subject.

An objective idea represents an object. A subjective idea represents by grasping an objective idea that represents. The intentionality of the mental act is therefore mediated by objective content. This is why Bolzano’s theory differs from Brentano’s later thesis that intentionality is the mark of the mental. Bolzano does not begin with an immanent object inside the act. He begins with objective ideas and propositions that can be grasped by acts.

The advantage is clear: different minds can think the same thing. Communication, disagreement, science, and proof become possible because mental acts are not sealed inside private consciousness. Two people can grasp the same proposition, even if their psychological images, emotional associations, and linguistic expressions differ.

The cost is also clear: the grasping relation is primitive. Bolzano gives us a powerful distinction between act and content, but he does not give a fully naturalized account of how a mental act comes to grasp this rather than that objective content. Contemporary philosophers would ask: what fixes the content of a mental state? Bolzano’s answer is structurally elegant but metaphysically incomplete: a mental state has its content because it grasps an objective idea or proposition.

7. Intuition, perception, and the inner basis of empirical knowledge

Bolzano’s account of intuition is one of the more subtle and difficult parts of his mind-theory.

An intuition, for Bolzano, is a simple and singular idea. It is simple because it has no parts; it is singular because it represents only one object. A concept, by contrast, is an idea that has no intuition as a part; a mixed idea contains an intuition.

At the subjective level, a subjective intuition is a mental occurrence that grasps an objective intuition. Examples include particular sensations or sense-data. But Bolzano’s view is not straightforward direct realism. The immediate object of a human subjective intuition is usually not the external thing itself, but a change in the mind caused by the external thing. In cases of outer intuition, the outer object may be the cause, but the proper immediate object is the inner mental event.

This gives Bolzano a kind of indirect realist or phenomenalist basis for empirical knowledge. We begin from immediate perceptual judgments involving inner givenness—judgments connected with “that” and “I.” From there we infer outward to enduring objects, causal relations, and the empirical world. Bolzano’s account of empirical knowledge therefore begins with private, immediate mental data but does not stop there.

The resulting view has several philosophical consequences.

First, Bolzano recognizes first-person immediacy. The subject has immediate access to certain changes in its own mind. The Athanasia also stresses that one is not immediately conscious of what another soul senses; consciousness is initially tied to one’s own mental life.

Second, external perception is fallible. If the immediate object is an inner mental event, then claims about external objects require mediation. The sensation may be caused by an object, but identifying the object involves judgment and inference.

Third, Bolzano anticipates later discussions of protocol sentences, indexicals, and the epistemic role of “I” and “that.” His analysis of empirical knowledge involves immediate perceptual judgments that use indexical reference before moving to more objective claims.

There is, however, a tension. Bolzano sometimes treats ideas such as [Socrates] or [Vesuvius] as pure intuitions, which suggests that some ordinary singular terms may express intuitions directly. But his more epistemologically cautious account says that human outer intuition immediately presents inner mental changes. Commentators note this tension.

8. Judgment, belief, knowledge, and inference

Bolzano’s philosophy of mind is strongly judgment-centered. The fundamental cognitive act is not passive representation, but judging: taking a proposition as true. A judgment is a psychical event in the real world. It arises, endures, and ceases. It may be caused by perception, memory, inference, testimony, habit, or other mental processes.

Bolzano sharply distinguishes the mental process of inference from the objective relation between propositions. A person’s inference is a causal transition in that person’s mind. An objective argument or grounding relation is not a psychological event. The fact that one proposition objectively grounds another is different from the fact that someone happens to move psychologically from the first to the second.

This distinction is crucial. It lets Bolzano say that reasoning can be psychologically defective even when the objective relation is valid, and psychologically persuasive even when the objective relation is invalid. It also allows him to separate reasons from causes without denying that mental reasoning has causes.

Bolzano’s epistemology then has two sides.

On the objective side, a body of knowledge should ideally be ordered according to grounds and consequences. Explanation is not mere derivability; it is the arrangement of truths according to what objectively grounds what. Bolzano distinguishes logical consequence from objective grounding, and this difference matters for scientific understanding.

On the psychological side, actual finite minds rarely proceed with perfect objective order. Human beings have limited memory, limited attention, limited time, and limited resources. Bolzano’s applied logic therefore studies not only ideal proof but also the real conditions under which human beings come to know, teach, learn, and become convinced.

Specialist work on Bolzano’s philosophy of mind emphasizes this point: his theory of cognition is not a static theory of justified belief alone, but a processual theory of epistemic activity. It asks what is happening when an agent judges, infers, believes, knows, or gains confidence. It treats the mind as equipped with capacities or forces for representing, connecting, asserting, feeling, willing, and desiring.

9. Bounded rationality, heuristics, and epistemic virtue

Bolzano is often described as an extreme rationalist because of his insistence on objective grounds, exact definitions, and logically ordered science. That description is only half right. He is a rationalist about the ideal structure of knowledge, but he is also highly sensitive to the limitations of actual human cognition.

He recognizes that real thinkers cannot always reconstruct the complete objective order of grounds. They work under constraints of memory, time, attention, and available evidence. They need rules of exposition, pedagogy, heuristic shortcuts, and practical measures of confidence. Modern commentators therefore connect Bolzano’s applied logic with bounded rationality, reliabilist epistemology, and virtue epistemology—not because he literally belongs to those twentieth-century schools, but because he treats rationality as something finite agents must approximate under empirical constraints.

This is important for extracting a philosophy of mind. Bolzano’s mind is not an ideal Cartesian intellect transparently surveying clear and distinct ideas. Nor is it merely a bundle of associations. It is a finite rational system: capable of grasping objective truths, but dependent on psychological faculties, memory, attention, training, linguistic expression, confidence, and social correction.

He also gives a role to what one might call epistemic affect. Discussions of confidence, trust, common sense, consensus, and a “feeling for truth” show that Bolzano does not isolate cognition from noncognitive mental life. Feelings are not themselves proofs, but they can play a role in how finite agents manage uncertainty, persevere in inquiry, or rely on others.

This places Bolzano in an unusual position. Against crude empiricism, he insists on objective truths and grounds. Against pure formalism, he attends to real mental limitations. Against irrationalism, he subordinates confidence and feeling to truth. Against a purely ideal theory of science, he asks how finite minds actually come to know.

10. Feeling, desire, volition, and action

Bolzano’s philosophy of mind is not limited to cognition. He explicitly treats feeling, desire, volition, and thought as mental phenomena—adherences of an individual mind or soul.

The will is especially important because Bolzano’s ethics is centered on willing. His supreme moral law instructs the agent always to choose the action that, among available options, most promotes the welfare of all; and he says that “ought” primarily concerns willing. This makes volition a central mental category, not a peripheral add-on.

Bolzano’s action theory can be reconstructed as follows. External behavior matters morally, but it is not the primary locus of normativity. The morally assessable core is the agent’s willing: the mental act of choosing or resolving under a representation of reasons, consequences, and welfare. A bodily movement becomes an intentional action because it is connected to volition. Volition, in turn, is a real mental phenomenon borne by the soul and causally connected with bodily effects.

This gives Bolzano a sophisticated position on reasons and causes. Objective reasons, grounds, and moral truths are not themselves psychological causes. But an agent’s mental grasp of a reason can cause judgment, choice, and action. The objective order of reasons and the causal order of mental events are distinct but coordinated. That coordination is central to any serious philosophy of action.

Bolzano’s moral psychology is also universalist. His moral law gives equal consideration to all sentient beings. This matters for philosophy of mind because sentience, not merely rational personhood, is morally significant. The mental capacity to feel welfare or suffering enters the scope of ethical concern.

11. Religion, soul, and moral psychology

Bolzano’s philosophy of religion is relevant because his doctrine of the soul is not isolated from his moral and theological aims. He defines religion in terms of propositions that influence virtue and happiness. A doctrine is religious not merely because it is metaphysically interesting, but because belief in it can affect moral life. He allows that religious doctrines may have practical, emotive, and directive functions, and he often evaluates them by their contribution to virtue and happiness.

This practical orientation helps explain the Athanasia. The immortality of the soul is not simply a speculative proposition about an invisible object. It has moral and existential significance: consolation, hope, responsibility, and the rational interpretation of human life. But Bolzano does not merely say that belief in immortality is useful. He attempts metaphysical arguments for it. The result is a hybrid of rational theology, moral psychology, and metaphysics.

For the philosophy of mind, the important point is that Bolzano’s soul is not just a doctrinal posit. It performs several functions at once:

It is the bearer of mental states.

It explains personal unity across time.

It grounds the possibility of rational agency.

It supports the possibility of post-mortem existence.

It connects human psychology with a larger moral and religious order.

This is alien to most contemporary analytic philosophy of mind, but it is historically central. Much of the pre-twentieth-century philosophy of mind was philosophy of the soul. Bolzano stands at a transitional point where the soul remains metaphysically real, but the analysis of thought, judgment, content, and inference is already moving toward the later philosophy of mind and philosophy of language.

12. Bolzano’s mind-body theory

Bolzano’s mind-body theory can be described as substance-interactionist, anti-materialist, and non-Cartesian.

It is substance-interactionist because the soul is a real simple substance and the body is an aggregate of substances, and they interact.

It is anti-materialist because mental phenomena are not reducible to bodily aggregates.

It is non-Cartesian because the distinction between soul and body is not a distinction between two entirely different kinds of substance, thinking substance and extended substance. Both are handled within one ontology of substances, adherences, forces, and aggregates.

The body matters deeply. Bolzano does not treat finite cognition as disembodied in ordinary life. Actual human minds depend on bodily and environmental conditions for perception, memory, communication, and action. Specialist commentary therefore describes his view as one in which actual minds are embodied and constrained by physical supports.

The body is not merely a prison or vehicle. It is the organized aggregate through which the soul receives sensory changes and produces action. Sensation is not explained as direct intellectual apprehension of external objects; it is a causal process involving outer things, bodily mediation, and inner mental change. Volition likewise is not merely an abstract preference; it is connected to bodily action.

From a contemporary perspective, Bolzano’s account has both virtues and weaknesses. The virtue is that he avoids making the mind a ghostly spectator; the soul is embedded in causal relations. The weakness is that his theory of “forces” and simple substances lacks the explanatory precision that modern neuroscience or physics would demand. It is metaphysically systematic, but not empirically developed in the modern sense.

13. Consciousness and first-person access

Bolzano does not have a “hard problem of consciousness” in the contemporary sense, nor does he develop a full theory of phenomenal qualia. Still, he has several doctrines relevant to consciousness.

First, mental phenomena are private in the sense that they are adherences of individual minds. My sensation, judgment, or desire belongs to my soul; another person may infer it, but does not immediately have it.

Second, immediate perceptual judgments are first-personal. They involve inner mental changes and indexical reference. This gives Bolzano a place for self-consciousness without making the self a Kantian transcendental condition of all objectivity.

Third, sensations and subjective intuitions provide the immediate basis for empirical cognition. Bolzano’s theory is therefore not purely intellectualist. Sensory consciousness is epistemically basic, even if external-object knowledge requires inference.

Fourth, Bolzano’s soul metaphysics gives consciousness a bearer. Consciousness is not a free-floating stream; it belongs to a simple substance. Personal identity is therefore grounded primarily in the persistence of the soul, not in the continuity of bodily matter or psychological memory alone.

There is a limitation here. Bolzano is far stronger on intentional content, judgment, and epistemic structure than on phenomenology in the narrow sense. He does not give a rich description of lived experience comparable to later phenomenologists. His most modern-looking contribution is not a theory of qualia, but a theory of how mental acts relate to objective contents.

14. A systematic reconstruction: Bolzano’s philosophy of mind in theses

The following theses extract a comprehensive philosophy of mind from Bolzano’s writings.
1. The mind is a real individual substance, not a bundle of ideas. It is the soul, or in rational beings the rational soul/spirit.
2. Mental states are real properties or adherences of that soul. Thoughts, feelings, desires, volitions, and judgments are not abstract entities; they are actual occurrences in individual minds.
3. Objective contents are not mental states. Propositions in themselves and ideas in themselves are independent of being thought, judged, spoken, or written.
4. Intentionality is mediated by objective content. A subjective mental act is about something because it grasps an objective idea or proposition that represents something.
5. The grasping relation is primitive. It connects the psychological realm with the objective logical realm but is not itself a causal relation.
6. Judgment is distinct from mere representation. To judge is to take a proposition as true.
7. Inference has both an objective and a subjective side. Objective grounding and consequence relations are not psychological events; actual reasoning is a causal process in a finite mind.
8. Perception begins with inner mental changes. External objects cause sensations, but immediate subjective intuition concerns changes in the mind; external knowledge is mediated by judgment and inference.
9. Human cognition is finite and bounded. Memory, time, attention, language, pedagogy, confidence, and heuristic rules matter for actual knowledge.
10. The will is a mental phenomenon and the primary locus of moral “ought.” Moral action depends on willing under representations of reasons and consequences.
11. Body and soul interact. Bolzano rejects both reductive materialism and Leibnizian windowless monads.
12. The soul is simple and, according to Bolzano’s intended metaphysics, immortal. This allows for disembodied finite minds, though the argument for immortality is philosophically vulnerable.
13. Mental life is broader than human theoretical cognition. Sensation, feeling, desire, volition, and perhaps nonhuman organic souls form part of the wider picture.
14. Philosophy of mind must be coordinated with logic, metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, and religion. Bolzano’s mind is not an isolated psychological machine; it is a truth-grasping, judging, willing, embodied, morally responsible soul.

15. Historical position

Bolzano’s historical importance is easy to underestimate because he does not fit the standard story.

He comes after Descartes, Leibniz, Locke, Hume, and Kant, but before Brentano, Frege, Husserl, Russell, and analytic philosophy of language. He is late enough to inherit problems about mind, representation, judgment, and empirical knowledge; early enough that he still writes within a metaphysics of soul, substance, and immortality.

In relation to Descartes, Bolzano retains the soul as a real bearer of mental life, but he does not simply repeat Cartesian two-substance dualism. His ontology is more Leibnizian and interactionist.

In relation to Leibniz, Bolzano inherits the language of simple substances or monads, but he rejects windowless monads and pre-established harmony. Finite substances interact.

In relation to Kant, Bolzano rejects the psychologizing or subjectivizing of logic and mathematics. Truths in themselves do not depend on the forms of human intuition or the acts of the subject. This is one reason he becomes so important for later anti-psychologism.

In relation to Brentano, Bolzano anticipates the centrality of representation and judgment, but he does not define the mental primarily by intentional inexistence. His focus is the relation between subjective acts and objective contents.

In relation to Frege and Husserl, Bolzano is a major precursor. His separation of proposition, sentence, and mental act foreshadows Fregean and Husserlian anti-psychologism. He is often treated as one of the most important figures between Leibniz and Frege, and his logic has been compared with later developments in Tarski and Carnap.

In relation to contemporary philosophy of mind, Bolzano anticipates several themes: propositional content, mental acts, belief versus mere entertaining, intentionality, the normativity of judgment, perception as mediated by inner states, mental causation, embodied cognitive limitation, and the distinction between reasons and causes. Modern historical work now treats him as part of the nineteenth-century history of philosophy of mind; Routledge’s History of the Philosophy of Mind includes a chapter specifically on “Bolzano’s Philosophy of Mind and Action.”

His historical position is therefore that of a hinge figure. He belongs backward to the metaphysics of soul and substance, but forward to the philosophy of content, intentionality, judgment, and objective meaning.

16. Comparison with major positions in philosophy of mind

Bolzano is not a physicalist. The mind is not identical with the brain or reducible to bodily processes.

He is not a Cartesian dualist in the strongest sense. Soul and body are not two radically heterogeneous substances defined by thought and extension respectively. They are integrated into a wider ontology of simple substances, forces, aggregates, and adherences.

He is not an idealist. Objective truths and propositions do not depend on consciousness.

He is not a phenomenalist in the full sense that only appearances exist. But his account of empirical cognition begins from inner mental appearances or changes, making him epistemologically close to indirect realism.

He is not a psychologistic logician. Logic does not study how people happen to think, but objective relations among propositions and ideas.

He is not an empiricist associationist. He allows mental association and causal psychology, but he insists on objective grounding, a priori knowledge, and conceptual truths.

He is not a modern functionalist, but some parts of his view have a functional flavor: the soul is identified in living aggregates by its ruling role, and mental capacities are understood through forces or faculties for representing, judging, feeling, willing, and acting.

He is not a neutral monist in his own terminology, but some interpreters see his unified substance ontology and non-Cartesian soul-body relation as moving in a direction that later neutral-monist theories would also explore. The safer formulation is that Bolzano is a monadological interactionist with non-Cartesian, partly monistic tendencies.

17. Strengths of Bolzano’s philosophy of mind

Bolzano’s first great strength is his act/content distinction. It remains philosophically powerful. A mental act is private and temporal; its content may be public, repeatable, and truth-evaluable. This is still a core issue in philosophy of mind and language.

His second strength is the distinction between causes and reasons. Psychological inference is one thing; objective grounding is another. This helps explain how reasoning can be both a mental process and answerable to nonpsychological norms.

His third strength is his anti-reductionism. He refuses to reduce truths to thoughts, thoughts to words, or minds to bodies.

His fourth strength is his attention to finite cognition. He is not content with an ideal logic of perfect knowers. He also asks how real agents, with limited resources, come to know.

His fifth strength is the integration of cognition and agency. Judgment, belief, knowledge, willing, and moral responsibility are connected parts of one mental life.

His sixth strength is historical. He provides a bridge from soul-based metaphysics to modern theories of content and intentionality.

18. Problems and tensions

The most serious problem is the primitive grasping relation. Bolzano needs it to connect subjective mental acts with objective contents, but he does not fully explain how it works. Contemporary philosophers would ask whether it is semantic, epistemic, phenomenological, causal, or sui generis. Bolzano’s answer is essentially sui generis.

The second problem is the metaphysics of simple substances. The idea that the soul is a simple substance bearing mental properties is coherent within Bolzano’s system, but it is not easily reconciled with modern biology or neuroscience without major reinterpretation.

The third problem is the proof of immortality. Bolzano wants the soul’s simplicity to entail its persistence, but that inference is weak. Even if a soul is simple, it does not obviously follow that it cannot cease to exist.

The fourth problem is the mind-body mechanism. Bolzano rejects Leibniz’s pre-established harmony and allows interaction, but the mechanics of interaction among simple substances, bodily aggregates, and mental states remain obscure.

The fifth problem is the theory of intuition. His account oscillates between a cautious inner-object theory of human intuition and a more direct treatment of singular ideas such as [Socrates] or [Vesuvius].

The sixth problem is the limited phenomenology. Bolzano is excellent on content, judgment, and epistemic structure, but comparatively thin on lived experience, affective texture, embodiment as felt, and the qualitative character of consciousness.

These problems do not make the system unimportant. They show where Bolzano’s framework is strongest and where later philosophy had to move beyond it.

19. Final synthesis

Bolzano’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as an anti-psychologistic realism about content combined with a metaphysical realism about souls and a causal psychology of finite cognition.

A mind is a real soul-substance. Its mental life consists of adherences: ideas, thoughts, judgments, feelings, desires, volitions. These acts are temporal and causal. But what they grasp—objective ideas, propositions, truths, and grounds—is not mental, not linguistic, and not causal. Perception begins from inner mental change caused by the world. Judgment adds assertoric force. Inference is both a psychological transition and, when correct, a grasp of objective relations. Knowledge is the successful achievement of finite rational beings who must operate under conditions of bodily dependence, limited memory, limited time, uncertainty, and moral responsibility. The soul may persist beyond the body, and Bolzano’s metaphysics is explicitly designed to defend that possibility, though the proof is not compelling by contemporary standards.

Historically, Bolzano stands between the old philosophy of the soul and the modern philosophy of intentional content. He retains soul, substance, immortality, and divine reality; yet he anticipates Fregean propositions, Husserlian anti-psychologism, act/content distinctions, theories of propositional attitude, bounded rationality, and the distinction between reasons and causes.

The best one-sentence extraction is this: for Bolzano, the mind is an embodied but potentially immortal rational soul whose real mental acts grasp nonmental objective contents, judge them as true or false, infer according to or against objective grounds, and will under moral norms.

PreviousThomas Brown NextWilliam Hamilton

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
